coredata.dc:description: Composite carbon quantum dots (CQDs), characterize as zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, play a crucial role in accelerating the separation of photoelectron-hole pairs within photocatalysts. Moreover, they introduce abundant oxygen vacancies (OVs) and facilitate the formation of new defect levels. Therefore, in this study, CA/BiOCl-x was synthesized via the hydrothermal method using carbon aerogel (CA) as the precursor for CQDs endowed with visible light activity. Through the degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH), the results demonstrate that CA/BiOCl-6 % exhibits the most prominent photocatalytic efficiency, showcasing a degradation rate of TCH under visible light irradiation fourfold higher than that of pristine BiOCl. Furthermore, the degradation activity is significantly enhanced under full spectrum irradiation, achieving a degradation rate of ∼100 %. The observation indicated efficient integration of CQDs into BiOCl through C = O, introducing abundant OVs. Electrochemical analysis revealed that the enhancement of catalyst recombination significantly augmented both the lifetime of photogenerated carriers and the intensity of photocurrent. Active species quenching experiments indicated the superior role of superoxide radical (•O2 −) in CA/BiOCl mediated TCH degradation. Liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry further confirmed the degradation pathways of contaminants. This study provides novel insights into the development of efficient photocatalysts for remediations of organic pollutants.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Composite carbon quantum dots (CQDs), characterize as zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, play a crucial role in accelerating the separation of photoelectron-hole pairs within photocatalysts. Moreover, they introduce abundant oxygen vacancies (OVs) and facilitate the formation of new defect levels. Therefore, in this study, CA/BiOCl-x was synthesized via the hydrothermal method using carbon aerogel (CA) as the precursor for CQDs endowed with visible light activity. Through the degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH), the results demonstrate that CA/BiOCl-6 % exhibits the most prominent photocatalytic efficiency, showcasing a degradation rate of TCH under visible light irradiation fourfold higher than that of pristine BiOCl. Furthermore, the degradation activity is significantly enhanced under full spectrum irradiation, achieving a degradation rate of ∼100 %. The observation indicated efficient integration of CQDs into BiOCl through C = O, introducing abundant OVs. Electrochemical analysis revealed that the enhancement of catalyst recombination significantly augmented both the lifetime of photogenerated carriers and the intensity of photocurrent. Active species quenching experiments indicated the superior role of superoxide radical (•O2 −) in CA/BiOCl mediated TCH degradation. Liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry further confirmed the degradation pathways of contaminants. This study provides novel insights into the development of efficient photocatalysts for remediations of organic pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: With the advancement of society, a substantial quantity of tetracycline industrial wastewater (such as reaction mother liquor and process wastewater) has been discharged into the environment [1]. Previous studies have detected over 220 antibiotics, including TCH, in the sediments of the Yangtze River with a total concentration of 11.88 ng/g [2]. Its prevalence as a contaminant in various aquatic environments has raised significant concerns for human society and the natural environment. Photocatalytic oxidations, holds promising prospects for applications in energy conversion, environmental remediation, medical antibacterial treatments, and chemical synthesis [3,4]. Among the many photocatalysts, due to its unique layered structure [5], and good stability [6], bismuth oxychloride (BiOCl) has been widely employed in photocatalysis and organic matter degradation. However, its wide band gap and high e−/h+ recombination rate hinder its practical application [7]. Various strategies have been explored to address these limitations, such as refrain the catalyst size at the nanoscale level [8,9], involving doping different elements [10], controlling the direction of crystal plane growth [11], fabricating heterojunctions [12] and introducing surface oxygen vacancies (OVs) [13]. The nanocomposites prepared by these strategies can generate a variety of active sites with well-defined coordination configurations [14]. Among them, the introduction of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to augment the content of the surface oxygen vacancies (OVs) is a common and effective modification strategy.

The presence of CQDs accelerated the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Through its unique up-conversion photoluminescence characteristics, it can enhance the light absorption intensity of the material and extend the light absorption range to the visible range [15]. And involve holes and •O2 − in photocatalytic degradation reactions [6], ensuring high-efficiency photoluminescence and photochemical stability without environmental harm [16]. Oxygen vacancy (OVs), the result of oxygen atoms or ions breaking away from metal oxides, not only induce the formation of a novel defect level and narrow the BiOCl band gap [17], but also broaden the light response into the visible region [18]. So far, the impact of CQDs on OVs and the synergistic mechanism between the two in photocatalysis has been explored by previous research. The induction of OVs can enhance the catalytic performance of photocatalytic materials. For instance, biocarbon and other forms of CQDs (Bio-CQDs) are combined with BiOCl to produce a large number of OVs [19–21], in which the oxygen component of the BiOCl lattice is replaced by carbon and a denser band structure is prepared. CQDs can form C = O bonds with oxygen atoms at the BiOCl interface. However, due to the inconsistent initial carbon content and structure reported for biochar, it is challenging to precisely quantify the quantity and size of CQDs introduced into the BiOCl system. This ambiguity impedes the further optimization of the photocatalytic performance of CQD-BiOCl nanocomposites.

Carbon aerogel (CA) stands out among various carbon materials due to its simple preparation and stable processing technology, as well as its distinctive features, such as high porosity, lightweight, and a considerable specific surface area, stemming from its unique network porous structure [22]. The consistent quantity of CQDs plays a pivotal role in the photocatalytic process [23]. Compared with other carbon-based materials (graphene, C3N4, biochar), the CA processing procedure exhibits stability. CA undergoes a standardized preparation process, ensuring a uniform introduction of CQDs. This stability significantly mitigates uncertainties stemming from raw materials, thereby enabling the production of a reliable composite photocatalyst. Additionally, the colloidal particle diameter of the CA network ranges from 3 to 20 nm [24]. Consequently, utilizing CA as the precursor facilitates the formation of CQDs via green synthesis, thereby imparting the essential quantum size effect [25] and surface defects crucial for improving catalytical performance. However, previous studies predominantly employed CA as a substrate for photocatalysts rather than in the form of CQDs [26]. There have not been any studies exploring the use of CA in the form of CQDs and the underlying mechanisms.

In this study, CA was combined with BiOCl to facilitate the photodegradation of waterborne pollutants. CA was prepared through the pyrolysis of phenolic resin aerogels at a high temperature. It was integrated into BiOCl in the form of CQDs through a high-temperature hydrothermal reaction, thus enhancing the OVs content of BiOCl. The photocatalytic performances of CA/BiOCl composites in the degradation of TCH under visible light and full spectrum were evaluated. The study also included the detection of intermediate products using total organic carbon (TOC) and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) to infer TCH degradation pathways [27]. The proposed photocatalytic mechanism was based on ESR and active species capture tests.

The preparation of the CA was based on the sol–gel polymerization method. Only CA125-2–800-1 was used in this experiment. Firstly, A predetermined amount of resorcinol (R), 37 % formaldehyde solution cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), and deionized water were added into a glass beaker with stirring well at room temperature for 30 min, and then transferred into a glass vial, the vial was sealed and placed into an oil bath pot at 85 °C for 5 days, the obtained organic aerogel (RC) was cured at room temperature for 2 days, followed by 60 °C for 24 h and then 105 °C for 3 h in an oven. Finally, the dried RC was heated to carbonization with a set heating rate and residence time in a tube furnace. The molar ratios of R/CTAB were 125, the carbonization temperatures were 800 °C, the heating rates were 2 °C /min and 5 °C/min, and the residence time was 1 h. After cooling, the carbon aerogel was pulverized and the particle size < 0.45 mm sieve leakage. They were denoted as CA125-2–800-1.

CA/BiOCl-x was synthesized via a hydrothermal method [11]. BiOCl prepared 2 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O via the hydrothermal method, was combined with an equal molar amount of KCl and 32 mL deionized water. The mixture was stirred intensively for 0.5 h, followed by the addition of CA of varying amounts and further stirring for 2 h. After thorough mixing, the solution was transferred to a 40 mL polytetrafluoroethylene hydrothermal reactor, and the reaction was allowed to proceed at 220 °C for 12 h. Upon completion of the reaction, the reactor was cooled to room temperature. The resulting precipitate was washed repeatedly with deionized water and absolute ethanol, dried at a constant temperature of 60 °C in an oven for 6 h, and ultimately yielded CA/BiOCl with varying doping amounts, expressed as CA/BiOCl-x (x = 2 %, 6 %, 10 %). Here, x denotes the mass ratio of added CA to BiOCl. Notably, the preparation process of BiOCl remained unchanged, without the addition of CA. A detailed coloration of the two materials can be observed in Scheme 1 and Fig. S1.

A powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was performed on a D8-Advance-A25 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) at 40 kV and 200 mA. The morphologies were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Nova Nano SEM 450°), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200X) operated at 100 kV and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL-2100F) operated at 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was determined by Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer using Al Kα radiation. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of the material were recorded with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline Noxide (DMPO: 50 mM, 0.2 mL) on an electron spin resonance spectrometer (Bruker A300). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 5700 FT-IR spectrometer. The 500 μm X-ray spot was used. The base pressure in the analysis chamber was about 3 × 10−10 mbar. The material was subjected to thermogravimetric analysis (TG) using a synchronous thermal analyzer (Netzsch STA 449 F5) under ambient air conditions, with a controlled temperature range of 30–900 °C and an airflow rate of 10 °C/min. The surface properties, including the volume of the surface sample, pore volume, and specific surface area, were determined using N2 gas Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET, Bruker NOVA42001) analysis. The UV–Visible diffuse reflectance spectra were recorded with a UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Lambda-750S, Japan), set the wavelength to 200–800 nm with barium sulfate as a blank background. The steady-state fluorescence of the materials was tested using an F-7000 photo-luminometer prepared by Hitachi, and the carrier lifetime was measured using an Edinburgn FLS920 spectrometer manufactured by Horiba Scientific. Total organic carbon (TOC) was monitored by a TOC analyzer (Shimadzu, Japan).

Photodegradation experiments on TCH aqueous solutions are conducted in a specialized quartz glass reactor. A 300 W Xenon lamp (power: 2500 W/m2) is used as a light source and released at a height of 10 cm above the contaminant solution interface, stabilizing the reactor temperature at 25 ± 1 °C through a circulating cooling device. Then the CA/BiOCl-x powder is put into the reactor together with the contaminant solution. The process consisted of adding 40 mg of the prepared catalyst to a 50 mL TCH solution at a concentration of 20 ppm in the visible light range. At the full spectral range, 10 mg of the prepared catalyst was added to a 50 mL volume of TCH solution at a concentration of 20 ppm. After stirring the degradation system under dark conditions for 30 min, turn on the xenon lamp for irradiation, take out the same volume of sample solution (2 mL) at a certain time (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 75, 90, 120 min), and filter using a filter device with a pore size of 0.45 μm. TCH concentration in filtrate was detected with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV–Vis) at a wavelength of 356 nm.

The main aspects examined were impedance, Mott-Schottky characteristics, and photocurrent intensity. A standard three-electrode setup was constructed with a platinum plate electrode serving as the counter electrode, a saturated silver chloride-calomel electrode acting as the reference electrode, and conductive glass coated with the sample material functioning as the working electrode. The preparation process of the working electrode involved ultrasonication followed by even dispersion of a certain amount samples in a film solution containing 400 μL aqueous ethanol and 10 μL Nafion to form a suspension. Subsequently, droplets of this suspension were placed on ITO conductive glass with equal area using a pipette gun and left overnight for natural air drying to obtain an excellently adhered material coating. For impedance measurements, 5 mM K3Fe(CN)6 electrolyte was chosen. Mott-Schottky curves at different frequencies were obtained in 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution electrolyte. Finally, photocurrent curves under blocked/unblocked light source conditions were recorded for five cycles (60 s per cycle) at open circuit voltage using a 300 W xenon lamp.

In order to detect the specific active species in the photocatalytic process, hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide radical (•O2 −) and holes (h+) were investigated by adding trapping agents IPA, TEMPO or Na2C2O4, respectively. The method was similar to the former photocatalytic activity test with the addition of 5 mM of quencher in the presence of TCH.

The XRD spectrum of ordinary CEM, presented in Fig. S2a, reveals two characteristic peaks at 23° and 43° in the XRD pattern, corresponding to the (002) and (101) crystal planes of CA, respectively. This confirms that CA is a graphite-like microcrystalline carbon material. Fig. 1
a illustrates the XRD spectrum of pure BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-x (x = 2 %, 6 %, 10 %) composites. The BiOCl samples exhibit several characteristic peaks located at 2θ, corresponding to the crystal surface of BiOCl (PDF#06–0249), where 2θ = 11.99° and 32.53° correspond to (001) and (110), respectively. These diffraction peaks are also observed in all samples of CA/BiOCl-x, indicating successful synthesis during composite preparation with no impurities present in any sample. Furthermore, their characteristic peaks are strong and sharp, suggesting good purity and crystallinity. However, no significant carbon peaks are found in the CA/BiOCl samples, which can be attributed to the low concentration of CA in the composites. Notably, CA/BiOCl-x shows a distinct (001) characteristic peak, which gradually weakens as the content increase of CA increases. This suggests that CA interacts with BiOCl at elevated temperatures, inhibiting the growth of the (001) crystal plane of BiOCl. The ranges of the diffraction peaks (002) and (101) are also altered to 23.9°-24.4° and 25.6°-26.3°, moving to higher angles as shown in Fig. 1(b-c). These changes correspond to the increased involvement of CA, further indicating the interaction between BiOCl and CA [20]. As the CA content increases, the diffraction peak related to (002) gradually broadens, suggesting that CA influences the formation of the BiOCl crystal plane along the crystallization direction of (002) when compared to pristine BiOCl [28].

The FTIR spectra of BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-x (x = 2 %, 6 % and 10 %) in Fig. 1d provide further insights into the chemical composition. The characteristic peak at 531 cm−1 is associated with the Bi-O bond, while the absorption peak at 1384 cm−1 is attributed to the Bi-Cl bond in BiOCl [29]. Additionally, the peak around 3472 cm−1 represents water adsorbed in the sample, as illustrated in Fig. 1e, which is a magnified view of the characteristic peaks in the range of 1500 ∼ 1700 cm−1. Upon doping with CA, an enhanced characteristic peak emerges around 1615 cm−1. This peak is generated by the surface –OH group, indicating that CA doping increases the number of surface OVs.

The microstructure of the photocatalyst is further investigated by comparing CA/BiOCl-6 % with BiOCl. The morphology and microstructure of the synthesized samples were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms (BET). As depicted in Fig. S2b, carbon carbide aerogels (CA) exhibit a three-dimensional nanonetwork structure, with spherical particles interconnected on their surface. These uniformly sized spherical particles, approximately 20–30 nm in diameter, form a network structure with pores of approximately 20 nm in size, Conjunction with BET data, as depicted in Fig. S2c and Table S1, CA exhibits a porous nature, indicating the presence of abundant pores. Fig. 2(a-d) displays that both pure BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-x samples have a stacked and distributed two-dimensional nanosheet structure. By conducting a comparative SEM analysis of the thickness and width of BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-6 %, it reveals that the thickness of BiOCl ranges from 357-390 nm, with a width of 4.84–7.66 μm. In contrast, the thickness of CA/BiOCl-6 % measures 86–130 nm, with a width of 1.12–1.66 μm. This suggests that when CA reacts with BiOCl in the form of CQDs, it results in the formation of smaller and thinner nanosheets, as observed under the same conditions. Consequently, this leads to an increased optical contact surface area and enhanced photon absorption, improving light energy utilization. Combined with the XRD analysis in Fig. 1a, the morphology of CA/ BiOCl-6 % is slightly different from that of BiOCl, which is related to the inhibition of CA [20]. Simultaneously, it is noteworthy that as the CA mass gradually increased, the nanosheet structure of the material exhibited a progressive trend towards smaller size and thinner sheet thickness at CA/BiOCl mass ratios of 2 %, 6 %, and 10 %. By conducting BET analysis on BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-6 %, as depicted in Fig. S2d and Table S1, it is evident that the addition of CA leads to a marginal increase in the specific surface area of BiOCl while concurrently reducing the material's pore diameter. Additionally, unreacted CA nanoparticles were observed to adhere to the surface at elevated temperatures in Fig. S3. The presence of unreacted CA nanoparticles may hinder the photocatalytic efficacy of BiOCl by diminishing the photosensitive surface area available for BiOCl. This observation further supports the notion that the addition of 6 % CA in this experiment results in nearly complete transformation into CQDs, consequently resulting in the highest photocatalytic performance on BiOCl. Therefore, it is evident from the aforementioned analysis that the introduction of CA can indeed influence the crystal growth of BiOCl. Moreover, the significant interlayer accumulation facilitates the formation of cross-plane diffusion channels for photoexcited carriers, thereby reducing their migration distance. Simultaneously, the specific surface area (SBET) of BiOCl is enhanced, leading to increased contact area, active sites, and reaction substrates within BiOCl [30].

The energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) images results are presented in Fig. S4, along with the average mass percentage concentration (wt%) in Table S2. It can be observed that the addition of CA leads to the lowest Bi quality when the amount of CA added is 6 %. The wt% of C and O incrementally increased and reached their maximum value at CA/BiOCl-6 %. While the average atomic proportion (at%) in Table S3 indicated a gradual decrease in Bi content, accompanied by an increase in C and O content. At CA/BiOCl-6 %, the average value of C atoms reaches its highest. This observation, combined with Fig. S3, suggests that prior to adding CA, O primarily combines with Bi in BiOCl. However, upon adding CA at high temperature (as shown in Scheme 1), the CA interacts with O in the BiOCl solution resulting in an increase in C and O content within the material. Consequently, due to failure to combine with O, Bi becomes free and is eventually washed away leading to a decrease in its content within the material. As the amount of CA increases, numerous –OH groups on incomplete CA interact with Bi3+ during synthesis which promotes cross-growth of BiOCl nanosheets on CA fiber network [6]. Consequently, excessive amounts of CA may result in incomplete reactions or even coverage on the surface, leading to reduced contact area for photon energy absorption by BiOCl. This observed phenomenon provides evidence that the amount of added CA significantly influenced the photodegradation efficiency of BiOCl.

To identify the specific CA type involved in the reaction and further investigate the internal structure of CA/BiOCl-6 %, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was utilized, revealing the binding mode of multiple CAs to the surface of BiOCl nanosheets, as depicted in Fig. 3 . As depicted in Fig. 3(a-c), at higher magnification, several mutually perpendicular lattice stripe spacings were observed, corresponding to the (002) plane and (101) plane with a 0.34 nm spacing of the four-square structure BiOCl, as well as (110) with a 0.27 nm lattice spacing. The SAED diagram shown in Fig. S5 also demonstrate the exposure of the above crystal faces. Fig. 3d reveals a substantial number of quantum dots appearing on the surface of BiOCl nanosheets for decoration. Upon closer examination, it was evident that there was a substantial amount of granular material distributed on the surface of BiOCl, which is likely composed of CQDs. This conclusion is supported by lattice spacing analysis, as illustrated in Fig. 3e. The crystals with lattice spacing of 0.37 nm and 0.22 nm in CA/BiOCl-6 % correspond to the (002) plane of BiOCl and the (100) plane of graphitic carbon, respectively. Fig. 3f further confirms the presence of CA as CQDs in BiOCl, thereby proving the coexistence of BiOCl and CQDs [31]. Other crystal plane spacings, such as 0.48 nm or 0.52 nm, can be attributed to the changes in oxygen vacancies in BiOCl [32]. Fig. 3(g-k) displays the element mapping images obtained by EDS analysis. These images show a uniform distribution of elements, and the primary elements identified in the CA/BiOCl-6 % sample are Bi, O, Cl, and C.

Subsequently, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis is conducted to investigate the interconnection between CQDs and BiOCl. In Fig. 4 a, the XPS spectra provide insights into the chemical composition in pure BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-6 %. Fig. 4b shows two peaks representing Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 of 157.0 eV and 162.3 eV, respectively, indicating the presence of Bi3+ ions in the material. Fig. 4c focuses on the O 1 s region, revealing three fitted binding energy peaks for CA/BiOCl-6 % at 527.8 eV, 529.4 eV and 531.0 eV, respectively. These peaks correspond to lattice oxygen, OVs, and H2O on the oxide surface [6]. Notably, the percentage of peak area representing chemical OVs in CA/BiOCl-6 % is 18.67 %, while pure BiOCl exhibits only 12.27 %, indicating that the addition of CA effectively increases the OVs in BiOCl. Fig. 4d explores the Cl 2p region, showing two fitted peaks centered at 195.65 eV and 197.25 eV, attributed to Cl 2p1/2 and Cl 2p3/2 [33]. In the C 1 s region, as shown in Fig. 4e, pure BiOCl displays three peaks at 286.6 eV, 286.4 eV, and 288.7 eV, while CA/BiOCl-6 % presents peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.5 eV and 288.9 eV, corresponding to C-O, C = O and C-Cl [6], respectively. The percentage of peak area for the C = O peak in CA/BiOCl-6 % is 14.47 %, compared to 11.92 % for pure BiOCl. This indicates that during CA doping, carbon can combine with oxygen on the surface and form more C = O bonds. This is mainly because the C = O bond (0.12 nm) is shorter and has a higher bond energy (755.6 kJ/mol), compared to the Bi-O bond (0.1934 nm, 337.2 kJ/mol). Thus, carbon in CA can seize oxygen from BiOCl during sample formation and increase the concentration of OVs [21].

Next, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy tests are conducted to demonstrate that the incorporation of CQDs can enhance the generation of additional OVs from the material, as presented in Fig. 4f. A significant signal with a g = 2.003 is observed in both BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-6 %. Notably, the signal intensity of CA/BiOCl-6 % is stronger than that of pure BiOCl, indicating the presence of oxygen vacancies (OVs) on the nanosheets of CA/BiOCl-6 % [34]. Cooperating with XPS analysis, it infers that CA doping enhances the OVs in BiOCl.

The thermal stability of the synthesized photocatalyst was evaluated using TG, and the relationship between mass loss and temperature was elucidated, as depicted in Fig. S6. Remarkably, the thermal degradation profiles of CA/BiOCl and BiOCl exhibited notable similarity. Under atmospheric conditions, CA began decomposing at 350 °C, with almost complete decomposition observed at 680 °C. BiOCl nanosheets on the other hand, initiated decomposition around 700 °C, resulting in the formation of Bi2O3, Bi4O5Cl2, and BiCl3 within the temperature range of 700–800 °C [35]. Following thermal decomposition at 850 °C, approximately 66 % residue (by mass fraction) was retained by both BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-6 %. These findings underscore the excellent thermal stability exhibited by both BiOCl and CA/BiOCl even under high-temperature conditions.

The use of TCH as a designated contaminant was employed to investigate the degradation effects of BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-x photocatalysts under visible light and full-spectrum conditions, as illustrated in Fig. 5 . As depicted in Fig. 5a, TCH blanks exhibit negligible degradation under visible light and display remarkable stability. In an experiment involving the removal of 50 mL of 20 ppm TCH under visible light, the catalyst reached adsorption saturation after 30 min of adsorption under dark conditions. The adsorption rates of TCH on BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-x (x = 2 %, 6 % and 10 %) were found to be 1.19 %, 2.48 %, 3.07 % and 5.66 %, respectively, and the degradation rates after 2 h of visible light irradiation were 19.48 %, 68.09 %, 79.5 % and 79.14 %, respectively. To compare the photocatalytic performance of different samples, the corresponding rate constants (k) are calculated with Equation (1), which originated from the pseudo-first-order kinetic model.
(1)
ln
C t
C 0
= - k t

The photocatalytic reaction rates of BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-x (x = 2 %, 6 %, 10 %) are determined to be 0.0016 min−1, 0.0085 min−1, 0.0121 min−1, and 0.012 min−1, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 5b. The degradation rate of TCH by BiOCl is significantly enhanced under visible light, with the degradation rate of CA/BiOCl-6 % being approximately four times that of pure BiOCl, and the corresponding rate constant being six times higher than that of BiOCl.

In the presence of a full-spectrum irradiation catalyst and a 20 ppm TCH blank sample, the TCH photodegradation decreased by 8.32 % within 2 h under full-spectrum irradiation. When 10 mg of catalyst was added to 50 mL of 20 ppm TCH under full-spectrum irradiation, as depicted in Fig. 5c, the adsorption of BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-6 % reached saturation after 30 min of dark adsorption, and the adsorption effect became negligible. The photodegradation rate of BiOCl within 2 h was 76.76 %, while that of CA/BiOCl-x were 98.77 %, 99.28 % and 96.76 %, respectively. The photocatalytic reaction rate constants of BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-x, calculated using the pseudo-first-order kinetic model, were 0.0128 min−1, 0.0423 min−1, 0.046 min−1, and 0.0269 min−1, respectively, as shown in Fig. 5d. Notably, the photodegradation rate of CA/BiOCl-x was higher than that of BiOCl, with CA/BiOCl-6 % reaching a state of photodegradation equilibrium (96.55 %) within 60 min. Furthermore, the maximum rate constant observed for CA/BiOCl-6 % was 3.6 times greater than that of BiOCl. These findings indicate that, compared to pure BiOCl, the introduction of CA in the form of CQDs can increase the active groups in the photocatalytic reaction, resulting in an increased reaction kinetic constant and excellent degradation efficiency. Fig. S7 displays the change in the UV–Vis absorption peak of the visible light and full-spectrum photocatalytic degradation curves over time. The intensity of the main characteristic absorption peak of TCH gradually decreased until it remained constant. This suggests that CA/BiOCl-6 % has a high removal efficiency for CA/BiOCl-x. The observed phenomenon is also in line with the trend indicated by SEM. Therefore, CA/BiOCl-6 % is selected for condition experiment because of its optimal photocatalytic performance in this system. In recent years, numerous investigations have been conducted on modified BiOCl photocatalysts. Comparative analysis of the properties and photodegradation efficiency of other modified BiOCl photocatalysts has been presented in Table 1 . Despite variations in test conditions such as light source, catalyst load, and initial contaminant concentration, our CA/BiOCl-6 % exhibits enhanced light absorption intensity and range along with increased active species concentration due to the synergistic effect of CQDs and OVs. Consequently, compared to previously reported studies in the literature, it demonstrates remarkable efficacy for TCH degradation across the entire spectrum.

The reusability and stability of CA/BiOCl-6 % are demonstrated by its continuous cycling for 5 rounds in Fig. S8. Remarkably, even in the fifth round of experiments, the CA/BiOCl-6 % composite retained robust photocatalytic activity for TCH degradation, with the removal rate consistently exceeding 91 %. Additionally, the XRD pattern of CA/BiOCl-6 % before and after the cyclic experiment is depicted in Fig. S9. The XRD patterns at the beginning and after four cycles exhibit remarkable similarity, indicating the robust stability of CA/BiOCl.

As illustrated in Fig. 5e, the initial TCH concentration exerts a significant influence on photocatalysis. As the TCH concentration elevated, the photocatalytic efficiency correspondingly decreased to 99.28 %, 92.45 %, 81.90 %, 76.39 %, and 66.11 %. Elevated concentrations of contaminants can impede light and photon energy transmission. The pH value is a crucial factor that affects the generation of active species during the photocatalytic process. As illustrated in Fig. 5f, the photocatalytic efficiency of CA/BiOCl-6 % for TCH reaches its zenith when the pH is neutral. However, an increase or decrease in pH significantly inhibits the photodegradation efficiency. Notably, when the pH values were 3 and 9, the TCH degradation efficiency reached 93.77 % and 86.31 %, respectively. The probable reason for this phenomenon is that under acidic conditions, the catalyst undergoes partial decomposition, leading to a decrease in catalyst dosage. Concurrently, at high pH values, the adsorption efficiency improves, causing excessive TCH to adsorb onto the CA/BiOCl surface, thereby reducing the light absorption rate. This scenario is counterproductive to the generation of e− and h+, resulting in a subsequent decline of active species production (•O2 − and •OH).

Photoelectrochemical measurements encompass a range of techniques, including photoluminescence (PL) emission spectroscopy, photocurrent response, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and Mott-Schottky plot collection. These techniques enable further investigation into the migration, transfer, and separation of photogenerated carriers. The light absorption performances of both BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-x composites were assessed by UV–Vis DRS spectrometer, as depicted in Fig. 6 a. It is observed that CA exhibits high-intensity absorption within a specific wavelength range. In contrast, pure BiOCl predominantly absorbs light in the ultraviolet region, while showing limited absorption in the visible region. The incorporation of CQDs in CA/BiOCl-x composites not only enhance the absorption capacity of invisible light in the ultraviolet band and below but also extends the absorption edge into the visible light spectrum. Particularly, with the increase of the quantity of CA, the absorption capacity for visible light becomes more prominent [46]. These changes in the optical absorption properties align with a gradual shift towards a pinkish hue induced by the introduction of CA. Moreover, the presence of OVs leads to a slight redshift in the adsorption characteristics of BiOCl following CA introduction. This phenomenon can be attributed to the interaction of OVs, which occupy positions held by free electrons (•O2 −) and thereby reduce the energy barrier for vacancy formation. This process generates shallow donor energy levels located below the conduction band (CB) [47,48]. As illustrated in Fig. 6b, the calculated bandgap energies (Eg) using the Tauc method for BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-x (x = 2 %, 6 %, 10 %) are determined to be 3.34 eV, 3.27 eV, 3.16 eV, and 3.12 eV, respectively. These findings shed light on the optical properties and bandgap characteristics of CA/BiOCl-x composites.

PL spectra reflect the recombination efficiency of electron-hole pairs (e−/h+). In the case of pure BiOCl, it exhibits a strong and efficient recombination efficiency of PL e−/h+ pairs, as shown in Fig. 6c. The introduction of CQDs within CA/BiOCl-6 % leads to a significant reduction in PL intensity, which indicates that CQDs and OVs weaken the PL strength of BiOCl. A specific analysis shows that CQDs have a conjugated π structure, which accelerates the electron transfer and promotes the effective separation of photogenerated carriers [49]. This translates to a lower recombination rate of e−/h+ pairs in semiconductor materials, aligning with the findings of previous research [50]. Furthermore, the presence of OVs introduces energy levels that are capable of capturing photogenerated electrons, leading to the quenching of the photoluminescent signal. This effect serves to inhibit carrier recombination, ultimately resulting in an increased concentration of active species [47].

Photogenerated carrier characteristics were investigated by assessing the photoelectron lifetimes of BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-6 % with time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL). This analysis is illustrated in Fig. 6d. The emission attenuation curve is well-fitted to a double exponential function, as described in Equation (2) [6]:

The results of the calculations are presented in Table 2 . The calculated average fluorescence lifetimes were 0.16 ns and 0.99 ns for CA/BiOCl-6 % and BiOCl, respectively. This disparity suggests that the average fluorescence lifetime of CA/BiOCl-6 % is longer than that of pure BiOCl. It aligns with an improvement in the transfer and separation efficiency of charge carriers, further corroborating the inhibitory effect of CQDs and OVs on the behavior of photogenerated carriers, as supported by the results reported in previous research [18].

The EIS Nyquist plot, presented in Fig. 7 a allowed for a detailed analysis of the migration of photogenerated charge carriers. The arc radius within the EIS spectrum of CA/BiOCl-x photocatalysts is generally diminished compared to that of BiOCl. The observed minimum charge transfer resistance at the CA/BiOCl-6 % interface signifies the efficient separation and rapid transfer of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The carrier lifetime (τe) of the material can be obtained by evaluating the Bode plot (Fig. S10) and the following Equation (4):

The calculated τe values of BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-x are shown in the Table S4, and the carrier lifetimes of all CA/BiOCl are higher than those of BiOCl. The results demonstrate that the nanocomposites can improve the photocatalytic performance by reducing the photogenerated electron-hole recombination efficiency during the photodegradation process.

To further elucidate the band structure and the characteristics of photogenerated electron transfer in the materials, Mott-Schottky measurements were conducted on BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-x composites, as depicted in Fig. 7b. These measurements reveal positive slopes for both BiOCl and CA/BiOCl, suggesting that both the two are n-type semiconductors. Consequently, the measured flat band potential can be employed as the conduction potential. Based on the analysis, it can be inferred that the conduction band (CB) potentials of BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-x are −0.62 eV, −0.76 eV, −0.56 eV, and −0.53 eV, respectively. Furthermore, considering the correlation between the NHE and Ag/AgCl reference potential, the conduction potentials of BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-x relative to NHE were determined as −0.42 eV, −0.56 eV, −0.36 eV, and −0.33 eV respectively. Applying the formula EVB = ECB + Eg, the EVB values for these materials were calculated as 2.92 eV, 2.71 eV, 2.8 eV, and 2.79 eV (vs NHE) respectively [21]. Therefore, the specific band structure of these materials is shown in Fig. 7c. Considering the fact that the conduction band potential (CB) of BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-x is close to that of O2/·O2 − (−0.33 eV vs. NHE) potential, it is conducive to the generation of ·O2 − radicals [18]. Moreover, both BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-x exhibit a higher VB potential than the H2O/•OH potential (2.27 V). Therefore, any holes remaining after electron transition can react with H2O molecules to generate •OH [51]. This result is consistent with the ESR test result as shown in Fig. S11.

The photocurrent density strength was further examined as illustrated in Fig. 7d. The ranking of photocurrent density is as follows BiOCl < CA/BiOCl-2 % < CA/BiOCl-10 % < CA/BiOCl-6 %. Higher photocurrent density values correspond a to more effective separation of photogenerated electrons from holes. Thus, it is evident that the photogenerated carriers in CA/BiOCl-6 % exhibit the highest separation efficiency among all composite samples, corroborating the results of the PL analysis.

ESR experiments can serve as a valuable tool to validate the presence of reactive oxygen species in the reaction system. Fig. S11 (a-d) demonstrates the absence of an ESR peak signal under dark conditions, while characteristic signals corresponding to 1:1:1:1 DMPO-•O2 − and 1:2:2:1 DMPO-•OH were observed for BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-6 % photocatalysts upon light irradiation, indicating their capability to generate/transform •O2 − and •OH species. This means that •OH radicals are also present in BiOCl and CA/BiOCl-x photocatalytic processes. Moreover, the intensity of ESR signals increased with prolonged light radiation time, suggesting enhanced production of reactive oxygen species.

To further investigate the photocatalytic mechanism, radical trapping experiments were conducted using three different radical scavengers: Na2C2O4, TEMPO, and IPA of 5 mM. These scavengers were employed to capture h+, •O2 −, and •OH [52], respectively. The corresponding photocatalytic performance and degradation efficiency of CA/BiOCl-6 % are presented in Fig. 8 a. When no capture agent was added, the degradation rate of TCH reached 96.63 %. However, the degradation rates of TCH reduced to 81.33 %, 37.86 %, and 95.75 %, respectively, with the addition of Na2C2O4, TEMPO, and IPA. Combined with the above ESR analysis, it is evident that •O2 − is the most active species in the TCH degradation experiment. Additionally, h+ is the second active substance. This finding is very consistent with other studies on TCH degradation [21,53].

The Total Organic Carbon (TOC) test can quantify the carbon content of organic matter in water. The reduction of TOC content in an organic solution signifies a corresponding decline in the concentration of organic matter within the solution [53]. Therefore, it can be used to assess the residual organic pollutants in a solution following the degradation of TCH by CA/BiOCl [54]. As illustrated in Fig. 8b, the TOC detection data for the photodegradation of TCH by CA/BiOCl-6 % is presented. Under illumination, the TOC removal rate of TCH solution demonstrates a gradual increase, reaching 21.1 % and 25.9 % after 1 h and 2 h of exposure to visible light, respectively. Under full-spectrum irradiation, these removal rates increase to 31.7 % and 36.0 % after the same time intervals. These findings effectively demonstrate that CA/BiOCl can progressively transform the polymer organic matter TCH into non-toxic, small molecular inorganic substances, such as CO2 and H2O. Consequently, these experimental results corroborate the outstanding application potential of CA/BiOCl-x in wastewater treatment.

The HPLC-MS detection can explore the decomposition pathway of TCH during photocatalytic degradation [6]. The results are shown in Fig. 9 , where the mass peak at m/z of 445 is consistent with the TCH stock solution. Mass peaks at 418, 359, 312, 270, 228, 194, 116, 426, 410, 360, 343, 299, 271, 100, 481, 393, 373, 262, 186, 156, and 118 represent intermediate products generated after photodegradation, as shown in Fig. S12. By the spectra of HPLC-MS collected at different reaction periods, it can be inferred that TCH molecules may undergo further decomposition under illumination through three different processes: dealkylation, dehydration, and hydroxylation products (m/z = 418, 426, and 479) [55]. These intermediates are subsequently subject to further reactions with the active material, resulting in their decomposition into smaller molecular intermediates through reactions such as deamination, decarbonylation, dihydroxylation, and ring opening. Ultimately, these intermediates are mineralized into CO2 and H2O.

The Toxicity Estimation Software tool (TEST) can predict physical properties of chemical structures, facilitating the estimation of chemical toxicity. LC50 values for 96-hour Fathead mimmow, LC50 of 48 h Daphnia minnow, and 48-hour Daphnia minnow, as well as LD50 values for Oral rat, developmental toxicity and mutagenicity of TCH and its intermediates were assessed using this software (the specific data is shown in the Table S5). For Fathead minnow, the LC50 of N2 (0.47 mg/L), N4 (0.08 mg/L), N5 (0.13 mg/L), N6 (0.45 mg/L), N9 (0.27 mg/L), N10 (0.17 mg/L), N12 (0.61 mg/L), N13 (0.24 mg/L) and N14 (0.24 mg/L) was lower than that of TCH (0.9 mg/L), and with the deepening of degradation process, the generated small molecule products gradually transformed into non-toxic state. The LC50 value of Daphnia minnow for TCH was 5.44 mg/L. Intermediates N2 (3.6 mg/L), N3 (6.06 mg/L), N4 (2.99 mg/L), N5 (2.01 mg/L), N9 (1.99 mg/L), N10 (5.85 mg/L), N12 (1.32 mg/L), N13 (1.38 mg/L) and N14 (3.04 mg/L) showed toxicity, while the other intermediates were transformed into harmful and non-toxic states, significantly reducing toxicity. The Oral rat LD50 of TCH was 1524.04 mg/kg, with intermediates except N3 (991.45 mg/kg), N6 (593.46 mg/kg), N14 (342.72 mg/kg) and N19 (516.84 mg/kg) having higher LD50 values than TCH. While TCH exhibited developmental toxicity (0.86) and mutagenicity (0.60), most intermediates showed lower toxicity but were mutagenic. TEST results indicated that although some intermediate products remained toxic, prolonging the reaction time could reduce toxicity. Overall, photocatalytic degradation of TCH had a positive effect on reducing toxicity [56].

When subjected to visible light and full spectrum irradiation, the valence band (VB) electrons of the material acquire energy and transition across the width of the bandgap to reach the conduction band (CB), thereby inducing electron-hole separation (e−/h+) and subsequently reacting with O2 and H2O to generate active species. Therefore, the enhancement of photodegradation performance in CA/BiOCl composites can be explained via a potential mechanism. By increasing the content of OVs on the surface of BiOCl, not only induces the formation of new defect levels and narrows the band gap, but also extends the optical response to the visible region. Thus, when BiOCl is modified, CA exists in the form of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which bind to the BiOCl surface via C = O bonds. This interaction increases the number of OVs on the BiOCl surface due to local lattice deletion and dislocation generation, resulting in the creation of new energy levels between the CB and VB of BiOCl. The OVs act as effective electron traps, facilitating light-induced electron capture at their respective energy levels. These trapped electrons can then react with O2 to form •O2 −, while some electrons can be transferred to CQDs [19]. Simultaneously, CQDs possess unique up-conversion photoluminescence characteristics and high efficiency photoluminescence, enabling them to extend light absorption into visible range, generate more electron-hole pairs through increased photon adsorption, and accelerate active substance formation [57]. Furthermore, the addition of CA can influence the crystal growth of BiOCl. This addition promotes interlayer accumulation, which in turn facilitates the cross-plane diffusion for photoexcited carriers, ultimately reducing their migration distance. Simultaneously, the specific surface area of BiOCl is enhanced, leading to increased contact area, active sites, and reaction substrates within BiOCl. Therefore, in comparison to pure BiOCl, the CA/BiOCl-x composites exhibit several advantageous properties. These nanocomposites demonstrate a narrower band gap, enhanced light absorption, and superior efficiency in photocatalytic degradation. Therefore, it is beneficial for the degradation of TCH. In conclusion, we conducted a systematic investigation on the modified BiOCl to enhance the photocatalytic degradation of TCH by introducing the synergistic effect of CQDs and OVs. The CA/BiOCl-x nanocomposites prepared via high temperature hydrothermal method not only induce the formation of new defect levels and narrow the BiOCl band gap but also extend the light response to the visible region. When CA is combined with BiOCl in the form of CQDs through C = O, it leads to an increase in OVs concentration, facilitating efficient carrier separation and promoting easier generation of •O2 −. These combined effects contribute significantly to the outstanding photocatalytic degradation performance exhibited by CA/BiOCl-6 % towards TCH. Furthermore, potential degradation mechanisms and pathways were proposed, identifying h+ pairs and •O2 − as key factors involved in TCH removal. This study presents a novel perspective on the microscopic synthesis mechanism of CA composite photocatalysts, offering valuable insights into the photodegradation of organic pollutants in natural water bodies. These findings have significant implications for promoting the sustainable development and potential application of photocatalysts in industrial wastewater treatment.
